Some good news from U.S. Environmental Protection Agency!
issued Working Guidance Final Nanotechnology Reporting Record-keeping Requirements Rule, which become effective this week, August 2017.
This important rule establishes one-time reporting record-keeping requirements certain chemical substances when they manufactured processed nanoscale.
early January 2017 issued Final Rule with many improvements that asked public comments docket (see earlier blog summary).
closed loophole proposed rule that would have exempted nanoclays, zinc oxide, nanocellulose from reporting requirements.
This means public will have more information make informed regulatory decisions about these materials.
rejected industry arguments volume below which reporting would have been required.
Such threshold have exempted many nanomaterials which are, course, notoriously volume their extremely small size.
rejected industry's request exempt naturally occurring nanomaterials from reporting requirements.
asserted authority under Toxic Substances Control (TSCA) section 8(a).
This rule particularly with above improvements scientific transparency public disclosure.
However, regulations restrictions.
Therefore, must information collects under this rule inform policies that will protect human health environment from harmful exposures these small-sized chemicals.
More about rule EPA's website.
earlier blog loopholes.
first started working this rule 2009, and, although Rule moved slowly through regulatory process, nanotechnology not.
last decade (since 2005) received reviewed over applications nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes that look much like asbestos (see report Mass Lowell, 2014).
Nanoscale chemicals (nanomaterials) products from commercial sectors ranging from sports equipment agrochemicals clothing.
Increased concern potential health environmental impacts chemicals, including nanomaterials, consumer products driving demand greater transparency regarding potential risks.
that end, published results research using GreenScreen hazard assessment method show both hazards data gaps conventional silver nanosilver approved commercial uses (Sass 2016).
ability conduct hazard assessments like GreenScreens published depends reliable publicly available information.
EPA's Rule important tool gather relevant data nanomaterials inform hazard assessment, regulatory decisions, industrial product design development.
